subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Depression, Positive Psychology, and Treatment (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
This study examined whether a brief gratitude journaling intervention reduces symptoms of depression and increases life satisfaction in adults experiencing mild to moderate depressive symptoms.
Participants
Two hundred and twelve adults were recruited through online advertisements targeting individuals who scored in the mild-to-moderate range on a validated depression screening questionnaire (PHQ-9 scores 5–14). After screening, 198 participants were enrolled. Participants who reported a current diagnosis of major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder, or were currently receiving psychotherapy were excluded. The final sample included 98 women, 81 men, and 19 participants who identified as non-binary or gender-nonconforming. Ages ranged from 22 to 58 years (mean = 34.6, standard deviation = 7.3). The sample was 55% White/European American, 17% Black/African American, 14% Hispanic/Latino, 9% Asian/Asian American, and 5% Other.
Method
Participants completed baseline measures of depression severity (PHQ-9) and life satisfaction (Satisfaction with Life Scale, SWLS) before being randomly assigned to one of two conditions for a four-week intervention period. In the gratitude journaling condition, participants received daily email reminders prompting them to write at least three things they were grateful for that day in an online journal. In the control condition, participants received daily email reminders prompting them to write a brief description of their activities earlier that day (e.g., what they ate for breakfast, what errands they ran). Journals were submitted online and compliance was monitored; participants who completed fewer than 20 of the 28 journal entries were excluded from final analysis, leaving a final analyzed sample of 174 participants (88 gratitude condition, 86 control).
At the end of four weeks, participants completed the PHQ-9 and SWLS again. Higher PHQ-9 scores indicate more severe depression (range 0–27). Higher SWLS scores indicate greater life satisfaction (range 5–35).
Results and Discussion
The researchers hypothesized that gratitude journaling would significantly reduce depression scores and increase life satisfaction compared to the control condition. The results are shown in the table.

Mean Pre- and Post-Intervention Scores by Condition

| Measure | Gratitude Condition (Pre) | Gratitude Condition (Post) | Control Condition (Pre) | Control Condition (Post) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PHQ-9 (depression severity) | 9.4 | 6.1 | 9.2 | 8.7 |
| SWLS (life satisfaction) | 18.3 | 23.7 | 18.1 | 19.0 |

Statistical analyses revealed that participants in the gratitude condition showed significantly greater reductions in depression and significantly greater increases in life satisfaction compared to the control group. The researchers concluded that a brief, low-cost gratitude journaling practice shows promise as a supplementary strategy for managing mild-to-moderate depressive symptoms, though they noted that longer follow-up studies are needed.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies the research method as an experiment, referencing random assignment or manipulation of an independent variable.

required_elements: The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to either the gratitude journaling condition or the control condition.
alternatives.correct_example: The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to either the gratitude journaling condition or the control condition.

required_elements: This is an experiment because the researchers manipulated the independent variable (type of journaling) and used random assignment.
alternatives.correct_example: This is an experiment because the researchers manipulated the independent variable (type of journaling) and used random assignment.

wrong_examples: The researchers used a survey study. [Incorrect — the PHQ-9 and SWLS were measures within an experiment, not the study design itself.]
common_traps: Identifying the self-report questionnaires as the research method rather than the experimental design
Confusing an experiment with a longitudinal study because it used pre/post measures

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of depression severity as used in the study.

scoring_points.description: States a measurable or quantifiable definition of depression severity as operationalized in the study — specifically the PHQ-9 score, with higher scores indicating more severe depression.

required_elements: Depression was measured by the PHQ-9, a validated questionnaire, with scores ranging from 0 to 27.
alternatives.correct_example: Depression was measured by the PHQ-9, a validated questionnaire, with scores ranging from 0 to 27.

required_elements: The operational definition of depression severity is the PHQ-9 score completed at the start and end of the four-week intervention.
alternatives.correct_example: The operational definition of depression severity is the PHQ-9 score completed at the start and end of the four-week intervention.

wrong_examples: Depression severity means how depressed someone is. [Conceptual definition, not the study's operationalization.]
Depression was measured by the SWLS. [Incorrect — the SWLS measured life satisfaction, not depression severity.]
common_traps: Providing a conceptual definition instead of the study's specific measure
Confusing the PHQ-9 with the SWLS

parts.prompt: Describe what the data in the table indicate about the change in life satisfaction scores for the gratitude journaling condition compared to the control condition.

scoring_points.description: Accurately describes what the means indicate — that the gratitude journaling group showed a larger increase in life satisfaction (from 18.3 to 23.7, a gain of 5.4 points) compared to the control group (from 18.1 to 19.0, a gain of only 0.9 points) — AND explains what this difference indicates in the context of the study.

required_elements: Life satisfaction improved much more in the gratitude journaling condition (an increase of 5.4 points) than in the control condition (an increase of 0.9 points), indicating that the intervention had a meaningful effect on well-being.
alternatives.correct_example: Life satisfaction improved much more in the gratitude journaling condition (an increase of 5.4 points) than in the control condition (an increase of 0.9 points), indicating that the intervention had a meaningful effect on well-being.

required_elements: Both groups started with similar life satisfaction scores (around 18), but after four weeks the gratitude group's SWLS scores were approximately 4.7 points higher than the control group's post-scores, indicating the gratitude intervention was more effective at improving life satisfaction.
alternatives.correct_example: Both groups started with similar life satisfaction scores (around 18), but after four weeks the gratitude group's SWLS scores were approximately 4.7 points higher than the control group's post-scores, indicating the gratitude intervention was more effective at improving life satisfaction.

wrong_examples: The gratitude group had higher life satisfaction after the study. [No data cited and no explanation of what the difference indicates.]
common_traps: Reporting only the post-intervention scores without noting the pre-post change
Describing which group improved more without explaining what this means for the study's hypothesis

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline the researchers followed in conducting this study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies at least one ethical guideline actually applied in the study, grounded in evidence from the study description.

required_elements: Participants gave informed consent before enrolling in the study.
alternatives.correct_example: Participants gave informed consent before enrolling in the study.

required_elements: Participants with clinical diagnoses were excluded to protect them from potential harm — they needed more intensive treatment than a brief journaling intervention.
alternatives.correct_example: Participants with clinical diagnoses were excluded to protect them from potential harm — they needed more intensive treatment than a brief journaling intervention.

required_elements: The researchers screened depression level to ensure they did not work with severely symptomatic individuals who required professional care (protection from harm/beneficence).
alternatives.correct_example: The researchers screened depression level to ensure they did not work with severely symptomatic individuals who required professional care (protection from harm/beneficence).

wrong_examples: The researchers should have provided therapy to all participants. [Identifies what could have been done, not an applied guideline.]
common_traps: Identifying an ethical concern or flaw rather than a guideline that was followed
Proposing improvements to the study rather than describing applied ethical practices

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable, using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
parts.scoring_notes: If students claim the study is 'partly' generalizable, their evidence must support both a reason it is and is not generalizable.

scoring_points.description: Explains the extent to which the study is generalizable with a specific population claim AND specific supporting evidence referencing participant variables from the study.

required_elements: The findings may generalize to adults aged 22–58 with mild-to-moderate depressive symptoms because that was the target population and inclusion criteria.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may generalize to adults aged 22–58 with mild-to-moderate depressive symptoms because that was the target population and inclusion criteria.

required_elements: The findings may not generalize to people with severe depression or diagnosed disorders because these individuals were specifically excluded from the study.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may not generalize to people with severe depression or diagnosed disorders because these individuals were specifically excluded from the study.

required_elements: The findings may have limited generalizability to non-Western or non-White populations because the sample was predominantly White/European American (55%) and recruited through online advertisements.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may have limited generalizability to non-Western or non-White populations because the sample was predominantly White/European American (55%) and recruited through online advertisements.

wrong_examples: The study might not generalize. [No evidence and no specific population referenced.]
The study had a large enough sample to generalize. [Sample size is not participant variable evidence.]
common_traps: Asserting generalizability or lack thereof without citing participant characteristics

parts.prompt: Explain how at least one of the research findings supports or refutes the hypothesis that gratitude journaling would significantly reduce depression and increase life satisfaction compared to the control condition.
parts.scoring_notes: Partial credit (1 pt) for using evidence without full explanation, or explaining without a specific finding. Full credit (2 pts) requires a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND an explanation of how it supports or refutes the hypothesis.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (partial): Uses specific evidence from the study OR explains the relationship between a finding and the hypothesis, but not both in a fully developed argument.

required_elements: Uses a result from the table in relation to the hypothesis without a full explanation.
alternatives.correct_example: The gratitude group's PHQ-9 score dropped from 9.4 to 6.1, while the control group's only dropped from 9.2 to 8.7 — this supports the hypothesis.

wrong_examples: The hypothesis was supported. [No evidence and no explanation.]
common_traps: Citing data without connecting it to the hypothesis
Explaining the hypothesis without referencing specific data

scoring_points.description: 1 point (full): Uses a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND explains how it supports or refutes the hypothesis that gratitude journaling reduces depression and increases life satisfaction.

required_elements: The gratitude group's PHQ-9 depression score dropped from 9.4 pre to 6.1 post (a reduction of 3.3 points), while the control group dropped only from 9.2 to 8.7 (a reduction of 0.5 points) — supporting the hypothesis that gratitude journaling produces significantly greater reductions in depression than the control condition.
alternatives.correct_example: The gratitude group's PHQ-9 depression score dropped from 9.4 pre to 6.1 post (a reduction of 3.3 points), while the control group dropped only from 9.2 to 8.7 (a reduction of 0.5 points) — supporting the hypothesis that gratitude journaling produces significantly greater reductions in depression than the control condition.

required_elements: The SWLS life satisfaction scores increased from 18.3 to 23.7 in the gratitude group (a gain of 5.4 points) versus only 18.1 to 19.0 in the control group (a gain of 0.9 points), supporting the hypothesis that gratitude journaling significantly improves life satisfaction compared to simply describing daily activities.
alternatives.correct_example: The SWLS life satisfaction scores increased from 18.3 to 23.7 in the gratitude group (a gain of 5.4 points) versus only 18.1 to 19.0 in the control group (a gain of 0.9 points), supporting the hypothesis that gratitude journaling significantly improves life satisfaction compared to simply describing daily activities.

wrong_examples: The gratitude group improved more than the control group. [Too vague — no specific data cited.]
common_traps: Citing pre-intervention scores without referencing the change
Noting direction of improvement without citing specific values or comparing to the control group
